data.title: Harmony Biosciences: Four More Wakix Years, Nothing Visible Beyond
data.description:
Harmony Biosciences (HRMY) is not a broken biotech, not at all. Its key product is Wakix, the only non-scheduled FDA-approved narcolepsy treatment on the market. Wakix makes close to $1B in annual revenue. The company has a strong balance sheet. It has time and it has capital resources on its side. Retail discusses LOE (loss of exclusivity) ad nauseam, but as management reminds us—“not so fast.” Settlements with ANDA filers and a push for label expansion will keep the runway working for at least the end of the decade—maybe more.

So this isn’t just a simple patent cliff bear story at all. I am not bearish on Wakix. I am just bearish on the company’s intent and ability to turn their Wakix-generated cash into a durable biopharma platform.

Wakix Today: Growth, Cash, and Visibility

Wakix generated $868.5M in 2025 revenue, and management is guiding to $1.0–$1.04B for 2026—that's double-digit growth moving into blockbuster scale. Given its market positioning, no wonder that the patient base continues growing, with 8,500 average patients on therapy and ~400+ net adds per quarter in recent periods. This isn’t the profile of a plateauing asset.

The company also has a lot of cash. Despite increasing R&D and SG&A, the company remains profitable, with approximately $882.5M in cash, cash equivalents, and investments in 2025.

There is no loss of exclusivity concern in the near term. Harmony settled with 6 out of 7 ANDA filers, and that extends patent exclusivity to 2030, possibly beyond if granted pediatric exclusivity. The 7th filer, Teva, is still in litigation, and if Harmony loses, a generic drug can come in immediately. If not, 2030 is still the baseline. So that’s a small, residual risk here.

A key risk is that in the narcolepsy market, modafinil and amphetamines are still widely used due to familiarity, cost, and physician comfort. Its nonscheduled status gives Wakix a distinct advantage. But, first, it is not superior in efficacy for everyone. And second, competition from molecules like Orexin agonists is closing the gap.

With those caveats, Harmony is operating from a position most small biopharma never reach: a growing blockbuster-ish asset, nearly $900M in cash, and 4+ years of visibility.

So we shouldn’t assess Harmony based on what Wakix has done; it should be assessed on what Harmony has done with Wakix-derived cash.

What the Market is Implicitly Assuming

The market is already being conservative here. The stock trades at roughly ~11x earnings and about ~1.3x EV/sales (with enterprise value close to market cap given the net cash position). This reflects skepticism. However, there is not enough skepticism.

If the market believed Wakix was just a finite asset with no reinvestment success and a clear decline starting in 2030, the stock would likely trade even lower. So the market seems to assume two things. One, Wakix remains durable for several more years. And two, the pipeline has some value, not zero value.

In other words, the market is discounting the story, but not completely. This is where I have a problem. If Harmony fails to build or buy a viable asset, even this discount will not be enough. On the other hand, if they do, the stock is clearly cheap.

The Setup: A Strong Asset That Has Already Generated Real Cash

Wakix has gone from essentially zero revenue in 2019 to $868.5M in 2025, expanding steadily year after year:

  • 2020: $159.7M

  • 2021: $305.4M

  • 2022: $437.9M

  • 2023: $582.0M

  • 2024: $714.7M

  • 2025: $868.5M

This is growth with high margin:

  • 2025 operating income: $208.5M

  • 2025 net income: $158.7M

At the same time, the balance sheet shows net cash of approximately $600M, reflecting a strong financial position. They have generated several hundreds of millions of cumulative profits, and their balance sheet is like a fortress—all from a single asset.

But that is also its weakness. It had all this time and all this cash—and it is still dependent on that one asset.

Where Did the Cash Go?

So what did they do with all that cash? First, their internal R&D scaled materially:

  • 2021: ~$30M

  • 2022: ~$71M

  • 2023: ~$76M

  • 2024: ~$146M

  • 2025: ~$190M

This is a lot of investment over the years. But it has not much to show for it. The pipeline today still doesn’t have a clear late-stage winner, let alone a second commercially viable asset.

Yes, EPX-100 (clemizole) remains in development for rare epilepsies, but the progress isn’t overwhelming. Its mechanics is serotonin modulation, and the second asset, EPX-200 (lorcaserin), points to the baggage surrounding this mechanism. Once known as Belviq and approved for weight loss, it was withdrawn from the market due to cancer concerns. Given that EPX-100 follows a related pathway—and was purchased for only $35M—it doesn’t do a lot to reassure investors. Plus, it targets Dravet syndrome and Lennox-Gastaut, and while early data has shown signals of seizure reduction, they went from early, heterogeneous datasets directly to phase 3, so we cannot make strong predictions on phase 3.

Given their strong balance sheet, they could have easily supported a strong $2–3B strategic acquisition of a late-stage or commercial asset. That would have been an effective replacement of the Wakix revenue stream—while they had time. Instead, they have spent their time on an underwhelming asset. Even though the asset may have been cheap, their R&D spend has been anything but. A better, more expensive asset would not have needed much more R&D spend.

Valuation—What the Market is Assuming

Currently at $30/share, Harmony trades at ~$1.7B market cap, has 57-58M shares outstanding, and its enterprise value is ~$1.13B. So the stock trades at ~1.3x sales (EV basis), ~5x operating income, and 11x earnings. That looks cheap on the surface, but it is not.

Here, the market seems to be doing two things at once. One, it is valuing Wakix as a finite-duration cash flow stream. At ~11x earnings, Harmony isn’t being priced as a growing biotech but as one with a harvestable asset. The market seems to expect it to continue generating strong cash in the near term but to face eventual erosion, whether from LOE, competition, or pricing pressure.

Two, the market is assigning limited value to the pipeline. On the one hand, R&D spend is rising, but on the other hand, the market doesn’t seem to be applying any premium for future assets. At just ~1.3x sales for a highly profitable asset, the market is effectively paying only for that one asset, nothing for the future. Harmony has time and cash—but no clear path to replacing Wakix, which caps the upside. Harmony has time and cash—but no clear path to replacing Wakix, which caps the upside.

Risks to the Sell Thesis

I have outlined a number of concerns that lead me to a sell rating here; however, for a well-rounded view, I should also look at what could make me change my bearish stance.

First, EPX-100 could still surprise us. I have said that the data we now have is limited, but the ongoing phase 3 studies could come up with surprisingly strong seizure reduction data. If a clean safety profile is added to that, the molecule quickly moves from being speculative to a viable candidate. Given the high unmet need here, even moderately successful trials can support a strong product.

Second, I have said I am not satisfied with HRMY’s current business development effort. However, it does have a strong balance sheet. They do still have time. If the company manages to find a deal for a late-stage or commercial asset in CNS, things could still turn around. It may not be a cheap deal, but they have substantial cash and no debt.

Third, I gave a 4-5 year runway for Wakix, given the Teva situation and some emerging competition. The Teva ANDA discussion may get resolved in HRMY’s favor, and those orexin agonists may come up short in trials. Either situation could meaningfully derisk the runway.

Taken together, these factors highlight that the downside case is not without risk. The thesis ultimately depends on the assumption that capital deployment continues to underdeliver. If that changes—either through pipeline success, acquisition, or stronger durability of Wakix—the current valuation could prove too conservative.

Conclusion

Harmony is a strong, profitable single-asset company built on Wakix. Revenue, margins, and cash position are all solid. However, the company has not been able to buy or develop a credible replacement asset. This is reflected in the discounted multiples the market is applying to Wakix. It may look cheap, but it is simply being priced for inertia, not momentum.

Until there is clear evidence of successful capital deployment into a durable second pillar, I view the stock as fully valued. Sell.

data.title: Huang Isn't Planning To Wake Up A Loser
data.description:
You may have already listened to Jensen Huang on the recent episode of the Dwarkesh podcast. If you haven’t, I highly recommend you listen to the entire episode and not just the bits and pieces clips. This wasn’t the kind of episode that you want an AI to summarize either.

What made the episode so enjoyable to listen to is I thought Dwarkesh did an excellent job in eliciting Huang’s opinions on a range of topics that are hotly debated. Frankly speaking, I came to the conversation with a slight bias toward Huang’s point of view of selling chips to China but came away thinking Huang’s arguments weren’t convincing enough.

Here on MBI Deep Dives, I am more interested in microeconomic concerns such as competitive dynamics, moats, etc., as I consider the broader macro, geopolitical concerns are largely beyond my pay grade even though they can be quite impactful at times.

There were plenty of materials in the episode that were very much in my narrow microeconomics interest, even though most people are mostly talking about the debate around selling chips to China.

Speaking of competitive dynamics, the below excerpt on Huang’s opinions about Trainium and TPUs is particularly worth highlighting:

I would welcome Trainium to demonstrate their 40% that they claim all the time. I would love to hear them demonstrate the cost advantage of TPUs. It makes no sense in my mind. It makes absolutely zero sense. On first principles, it makes no sense.So I think the reason why we’re so successful is simply because our TCO is so great. Secondly, you say 60% of our customers are the top five, but most of that business is external. For example, most of Nvidia in AWS is for external customers, not internal use. Most of our customers at Azure, obviously all of our customers are external. All of our customers at OCI are external, not internal use. The reason why they favor us is because our reach is so great. We can bring them all of the great customers in the world. They’re all built on Nvidia. And the reason why all these companies are built on Nvidia is because our reach and our versatility is so great.So I think the flywheel is really install base, the programmability of our architecture, the richness of our ecosystem, and the fact that there’s so many AI companies in the world. There’s tens of thousands of them now. If you were one of those AI startups, what architecture would you choose? You would choose an architecture that’s most abundant. We’re the most abundant in the world. You’d choose the one that has the largest installed base. We’re the largest install base. And you’d choose the one that has a rich ecosystem.So that’s the flywheel. That’s the reason why, between the combination of: one, our perf per dollar is so great that they have the lowest cost tokens. Second, our perf per watt is the highest in the world. So if one of these companies, if our partners, built a one gigawatt data center, that one gigawatt data center better deliver the maximum amount of revenues and number of tokens, which directly translates to revenues. You want it to generate as many tokens as possible, maximize the revenues for that data center. We are the highest tokens per watt architecture in the world. Lastly, if your goal is to rent the infrastructure, we have the most customers in the world. So that’s the reason why the flywheel works.

Jensen correctly notes that Amazon (AWS) (AMZN), Microsoft (Azure) (MSFT), and Google (GCP) (GOOG) (GOOGL) buy mountains of GPUs primarily because their external cloud tenants demand them. Hyperscalers, simplistically speaking, are increasingly acting as capital-intensive distributors for Nvidia (NVDA). It may take a while, but I still wonder if, unless we get past this compute-constrained environment, the true competitive dynamics between ASICs and Nvidia GPUs may be hard to ascertain.

In the current environment, you will probably sign a contract with whoever can offer you compute. As a result, Trainium, TPUs, and GPUs all can coexist and thrive in this environment. I am noticing a growing consensus among investors that “we may never have enough compute,” which implies worrying about competitive dynamics in a relatively compute-abundant scenario is largely irrelevant.

I, however, lean towards believing in the power of capitalism, and there is hardly any shortage that capitalism has failed to eliminate over time. As a result, while it’s excruciatingly challenging to pinpoint a timeline when we will reach such a relative compute abundance scenario, I suspect we will eventually have to deal with such questions.

If you value these businesses, assuming such a question will never arrive, but it does arrive five years from now, that has much more valuation implications than most investors may imagine.

I continue to think that in a relative compute abundance scenario, TPU is the most protected precisely because Google has enormous captive internal workloads: Search query serving, YouTube ranking, ad targeting, Gemini across every surface, Waymo, and Workspace AI features.

Even if external TPU demand goes to zero, internal workload volume alone justifies continued investment and tape-outs. They’re obviously not immune, just less exposed than Trainium and Nvidia’s GPUs.

My guess is DeepMind would be very happy to get a higher compute allocation if Google Cloud faces any difficulty selling compute to external customers at an attractive ROIC.

Huang also made an interesting point about ASICs vs. GPU margins:

“don’t forget, even in ASICs margins are really quite high. Nvidia’s margin is 70%, let’s say. But ASIC margins are 65%. What are you really saving?You’ve got to pay somebody. I think the ASIC margins are incredibly good, from what I can tell. They believe it too. They’re quite proud of their incredible ASIC margins.”

I found this argument to be a bit misleading. Epoch AI suggests that Blackwell chips sell for $30k to $40k, whereas the bill of materials (BOM) costs only ~$5.7k to $7.3k, which implies an eye-watering 82% margin.

They, however, did point out that “Since most Blackwell revenue comes from servers and rack-scale systems, which may carry lower margins, NVIDIA’s realized margins on Blackwell sales may be lower than these chip-level estimates.”

When a hyperscaler pays Nvidia $30k+ for a Blackwell unit, Nvidia's margin is captured on the entire bill of materials plus the software stack. Broadcom's (AVGO) or Marvell's (MRVL) "65% ASIC margin" is on a much narrower slice. The hyperscaler pays TSMC (TSM) directly for wafers and pays SK Hynix, Samsung (SSNLF), or Micron (MU) directly for HBM, which together are the majority of the BOM on a current-generation accelerator.

Broadcom's margin sits on top of its contribution, not on top of the whole chip. Saying "65% vs 70%" makes it sound like near-parity, but the 65% is applied to perhaps a quarter of the system cost while the 70% is applied to all of it.

A hypothetical chip with ~$6k of real BOM cost sold by Nvidia at $30k captures $24k of margin per unit. The same chip produced through a design-services relationship with Broadcom might carry ~$8k of Broadcom margin plus the same ~$6k BOM, landing the hyperscaler at $14-15k all-in. Even if Broadcom's percentage margin on its slice is as high as Nvidia's, the dollar savings per unit are enormous because Broadcom's slice is so much smaller.

Custom silicon programs do, however, carry real costs that don't get surfaced in this simple framing. There is internal engineering headcount, inference and training framework support, and the opportunity cost of engineers who could be working on revenue-generating products instead.

These are fixed and amortized, but they are not zero, and for a program that fails to reach sufficient volume, the amortized per-unit cost can be meaningful vs. just merely comparing the BOM.

This is the actual reason most companies do not build custom silicon: because you need Google-scale or AWS-scale internal demand and a top-tier engineering team to make the amortization math work.

For the handful of operators where the math does work, the savings are not 65% vs. 70%. They are likely closer to single-digit thousands vs. tens of thousands per chip, and I’m sure Huang knows this.

However, there are different layers to this debate. Gavin Baker pointed out that while model portability used to make investors think model developers might gain leverage over the chip suppliers over time, that may be changing with Blackwell and even more so with Rubin. From Baker’s post (emphasis mine):

As system level architectures diverge (torus vs. switched scale-up topologies, memory hierarchies, networking primitives), true portability is eroding. The Mi300 and Mi325 had roughly the same scale-up domain size as Hopper while Blackwell’s scale-up domain is 9x larger than the Mi355 scale-up domain, etc.Many frontier models are now being explicitly co-designed for inference on specific hardware like GB300 racks. Codex on Cerebras is another example. Those models run less efficiently on other systems and the performance differentials will only widen. A model that runs well on Google’s torus topology will run less efficiently on Nvidia’s switched scale-up topology and vice versa - the data traffic is fundamentally different as a byproduct of the models being parallelized across the different topologies.Google’s internal teams - and increasingly the Anthropic teams as they become the most important customer of almost every cloud - have the luxury of operating across the stack (models, chips, networking) - but that is not the case for the rest of the market and other prospective users. Anthropic is the exception, not the rule. To wit, Anthropic and Google allegedly have a mutual understanding where Anthropic can hire the TPU engineers they need every year to ensure that they can continue to get the most out of the TPU.Given the overwhelming importance of cost per token to the economics of the labs, models will be run where they run best. Most extremely large MoE models will run best on GB300s given the importance of having a switched scale-up network like NVLink for MoE inference. When training was the dominant cost for labs and power was broadly available, labs were optimizing to minimize capex dollars. Model portability was a way to create leverage over suppliers. I think that drove a lot of the focus on portability.Today, inference costs as measured by tokens per watt per dollar are everything. Inference is way more important than training costs (inference is effectively now part of training via RL). Labs are therefore now optimizing for inference. This means increasing co-design and higher go-forward switching costs for individual models between systems. I do think this explains why Anthropic and Nvidia came together: Anthropic needed Blackwells and Rubins to inference at least *some* of their models economically. And Mythos might just end up being released coincident with the availability of Rubins for inference.TLDR: as labs shift their focus from training to inference, the costs of portability and the upside of co-design to maximize tokens per watt per dollar both rise. Portability is likely to begin decreasing as a result.

Reading Baker’s post made me think that the hyperscalers will have to pay the “Nvidia tax” whether they like it or not; as long as the hyperscaler customers see material benefit for using Nvidia GPUs over hyperscaler ASICs, hyperscalers' hands will be tied.

And if hyperscalers appear unwilling or hesitant to pay such exorbitant taxes, Nvidia will be happy to allocate more of their chips towards neoclouds, and many customers, especially AI startups will likely follow. Always remember, “You’re not talking to somebody who woke up a loser.”

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NZD/USD's 3-Day Decline Ends, Potential Bullish Reversal Above 0.5846 Key Support
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

Annual inflation in New Zealand came in at 3.1% year-on-year in Q1 2026, unchanged from Q4 2026’s 1.5-year high but exceeded the consensus forecast of 2.9%.

The latest inflation print in New Zealand has continued to surpass the RBNZ (New Zealand central bank) long-term inflation target of 1%-3%, therefore increasing the odds of a 25 basis points (bps) interest rate hike by the RBNZ in July’s monetary policy meeting to bring the official cash policy rate higher to 2.50%. So far, the RBNZ has kept its policy rate unchanged at 2.25% for two consecutive meetings since February 2026.

2-year NZ sovereign bond/US Treasury yield spread has started to price in a more hawkish RBNZ

By looking at the current 2-year yield spread between New Zealand sovereign bonds and US Treasuries from a technical analysis perspective, it has traced out a major bullish reversal “Inverse Head & Shoulders” configuration since January 9, 2025 and traded above its 200-day moving average, which is acting as a key support at -0.45% (see Fig. 1).

Therefore, breaking above the neckline resistance of the “Inverse Head & Shoulders” at -0.09% is likely to see a further rally in the current 2-year yield spread between New Zealand sovereign bonds and US Treasuries (US Treasuries’ yield premium shrinkage), in turn, putting potential upside pressure on the NZD/USD rate.

Let us now examine the short-term outlook (1-3 days) of NZD/USD from a technical analysis perspective.

NZD/USD - Bullish reversal at 0.5846 support

Watch the 0.5880/0.5846 key short-term pivotal support on the NZD/USD. A clearance above 0.5929 opens scope for a further potential short-term rally for the next intermediate resistances to come in at 0.5965 and 0.6015/0.6030 (also a Fibonacci extension) (see Fig. 2).

However, failure to hold and an hourly close below 0.5846 invalidates the bullish scenario for a minor corrective pull-back to retest the 20-day moving average that is acting as the next intermediate support at 0.5800. A break below 0.5800 may trigger a deeper slide to expose 0.5725 next.

Key elements to support the near-term bullish bias on NZD/USD

  • Price actions of NZD/USD have continued to oscillate within a minor ascending channel in place since the April 7, 2026 low of 0.5690 and still have room to maneuver towards the upper boundary of the minor ascending channel (see Fig. 2).
  • NZD/USD has just shaped a 3-day (April 17, April 21, and April 22) bullish reversal candlestick condition on the retest of its key 200-day moving average, indicating the potential end of the minor corrective decline sequence from April 15, 2026 to April 20, 2026 (see Fig. 3).
  • The daily RSI momentum indicator has shaped a higher low above the 50 level and has not reached its overbought region (above the 70 level) (see Fig. 3).

Original Post

data.title: Steel Dynamics: Tariff Benefits Strengthen In Q1
data.description:
Shares of Steel Dynamics (STLD) have been a remarkable performer over the past year, gaining over 80%. While it has taken several quarters, the steel tariffs have helped to firm up pricing in the sector, supporting profitability. At the same time, its transformational aluminum plant continues to progress, enabling a coming inflection in cash flow. While the company reported solid Q1 results, there were signs that broader U.S. manufacturing activity remains muted. I last covered shares in January, rating the stock a “Hold” and recommending buying on pullbacks to $170, which materialized in March. Considering shares are back over $200, this proved to be a good entry point, and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit STLD.

In the company’s first quarter, Steel Dynamics earned $2.78 per share, which was $0.02 below expectations even as revenue jumped 19% to $5.2 billion. Earnings nearly doubled from last year’s $1.44, reflecting higher pricing, thanks in large part to the protection afforded the industry by tariffs. During the quarter, STLD generated $700 million of EBITDA. Steel shipments were a record 3.6 million tons. As you can see below, its utilization is up to 89%, continuing to outpace the industry. The share of imports has fallen significantly to just 15%, reflecting tariffs. This is enabling the domestic industry to regain share and boost pricing, a tailwind to STLD’s bottom line.

Now, during the first quarter, the Supreme Court overruled many of the Trump Administration’s “reciprocal” tariffs. It is important to emphasize that this does not impact steel tariffs. Steel and aluminum tariffs were installed under a different legal authority, Section 232. This was the same section used in the first Trump Administration for tariffs and provides much stronger legal footing.

I view these tariffs as highly likely to endure at least through the duration of the Trump Administration and likely to remain to a degree even after that, given the Biden Administration retained some steel tariffs, speaking to their bipartisan support. Frankly, in my view, the ideal outcome for the steel industry is one where steel is tariffed, insulating it from foreign competition, while most other industries face lower tariffs to minimize any demand headwinds from widespread tariffs. That is the outcome we are moving towards with the Supreme Court’s limitation of broad tariff policy while Section 232 actions remain intact.

Looking at segment results, its Steel Operations earned $557 million, up from $230 million last year. Its average selling price increased by 20% to $1,193, while input costs were up just 3% to $396. Tariffs have helped to reduce foreign competition, tighten the market, and boost prices. Beyond this, volumes were up 5% from last year, reflecting the fact that STLD is not just adding pricing but also gaining market share. Management saw steel demand strengthen during the quarter with backlogs growing thanks to solid customer orders. I expect results to remain strong.

While its purely commodity business did very well, its value-add fabrication unit saw earnings fall 23% from last year to $90 million. Here, prices were actually down 5% to $2,478, though volumes were up 6%, and it has not been able to fully pass on higher commodity costs. Still, orders were strong, with the backlog up 38% from last year and extending into October, aided by data center demand. With nonresidential construction activity still running lower than in late 2023, I expect fabricated demand to remain low and for margin trends to be weaker than on the commodity side of the business.

Finally, its aluminum business lost $65 million, more than doubling last year’s $29 million loss. This was to be expected as it ramps up staffing and operations ahead of the full launch of the plant. During the quarter, it completed finished products, another key benchmark. The plant began production last June, and it should continue to ramp up. Aluminum is one of the more energy-intensive metals, and with U.S. natural gas relatively inexpensive, this should further support the facility’s relative economics beyond even just the tariff benefit. Over time, I expect this unit to provide over $650 million of EBITDA while helping to diversify STLD away from just steel, creating a more resilient business.

Now, cash flow was a bit weak, reflecting a retirement profit-sharing distribution of $120 million. As is typically the case, working capital was a significant seasonal headwind in Q1, consuming about $459 million of cash flow. This working capital movement should largely reverse over the balance of the year. Meanwhile, cap-ex was down by more than half to $138 million, reflecting the fact that the company has passed peak cap-ex with the aluminum mill moving into production. This should enable free cash flow to inflect significantly positively this year.

Steel Dynamics maintains a solid balance sheet, which enables it to return capital to shareholders. Leverage stands at 1.4x EBITDA. Now, its leverage has risen from essentially zero a few years ago, reflecting the normalization of EBITDA from post-COVID extremes as well as its investments in significant growth projects and cap-ex. Given the cyclicality of the business, it is important to maintain a strong balance, and I am comfortable with leverage running up to 2x. I expect capital returns to be largely funded out of cash flow this year.

Earlier this year, the company was involved with an effort to buy BlueScope Steel, where it would take ownership of its U.S. operations. However, BlueScope declined the best and final bid. At this time, I do not expect STLD to complete a deal, but there is clearly a willingness to use its balance sheet to further expand operations if opportunities were to arise. During the quarter, the company bought back $115 million of stock. STLD also pays a 1.1% dividend yield. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 3.5% from last year. I anticipate about $500 million of buybacks this year.

Back in January, I was targeting $12-$13 in EPS. With stronger steel pricing and the aluminum business likely to inflect in H2 as activity ramps, I now see $13-$14 in EPS this year. I remain a bit more cautious than the consensus of $14.03, as energy inflation may pressure margins modestly. Plus, I remain more concerned that energy disruptions could prove larger than the relatively sanguine market outlook, which may ultimately weigh on economic activity and reduce demand.

Shares are now trading at ~15.5x earnings, which is fairly full. That said, its aluminum facility should provide continued growth through 2028, and I expect a capital return yield of 5+% this year. There is also the potential that tariffs provide further pricing pressure and that my view is a bit too cautious. As such, I do not see a need to sell STLD, given its strong positioning and growth project timing. I view shares as fairly valued and remain a “Hold,” though I would wait for pullbacks to ~$190 before adding more shares given some downside economic risks.

data.title: Zions Bancorporation: Q1 Results Affirm The Strength Of Its Underwriting
data.description:
Shares of Zions Bancorporation (ZION) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining about 45%. Shares have shrugged off concerns in the fall about losses on some private credit exposure, as its decentralized community model has otherwise kept results at a strong level. On Monday, Zions reported solid Q1 results that further affirmed its strengths as well as showing ongoing balance sheet mix improvement. I last covered Zions in October when I rated the stock a "Buy." Since then, shares have gained 12%, outpacing the market’s 7% rally, approaching my $65 price target. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit shares.

In the company’s first quarter, Zions Bancorporation earned $1.56 per share, which beat estimates by $0.13 as adjusted revenue grew 13%. As a reminder, Zions operates across the western portion of the United States. Within this footprint, it operates a fairly unique model where it owns multiple community banks, which are run in a decentralized fashion. While this approach reduces economies of scale, it provides for closer community contact, which should result in lower loan losses over time.

Looking first at its balance sheet, deposits are up about 1% from last year at $75.5 billion. While total deposits are stable, it has seen significant mix shift improvement, with non-interest-bearing (“NIB”) balances up $2 billion from last year. In this still-elevated rate environment, these balances are very valuable sources of funding. This growth has allowed ZION to let $1 billion of high-cost brokered deposits roll off over the past year while also reducing expensive wholesale borrowing. These factors have helped drive down funding costs.

As has been the case for much of the industry, loan growth has been sluggish, running at about 2.5% over the past year. Loan yields fell 14 bps sequentially, reflecting fed funds rate cuts, as most loans are floating rate. Within this portfolio, it has $14 billion of commercial real estate exposure (“CRE”), but just $1.6 billion of this is in office. Across its portfolio, loan-to-value ratios are below 60%, and only 0.2% are currently delinquent. I continue to believe the worst has passed this sector.

Within loan portfolios, exposure to “non-depository financial institutions” (“NDFI”) has been a key focus as investors gauge exposure to problems in private credit. Zions came into the crosshairs for a bit last fall when it reported a $60 million loss. Frankly, given its size, this is not that material of a loss, but investors were afraid it could be the sign of things to come. In reality, its NDFI exposure is quite low at just 3% of loans. Some of this exposure, like to mortgage credit, is quite low risk.

That said, it does have about $974 million in business credit lending, including to BDCs. This is where the greatest exposure to losses sits, in my view. Currently, Zions’ NDFI portfolio is strong, with just 0.7% of loans classified, adding to my view that its October loss was a one-off misstep rather than a sign of systemic underwriting problems. Even assuming a 50% loss rate on business credit lending and a 50% loss given default, the after-tax impact would be about $1.30/share, less than one quarter’s earnings. While that would obviously not be pleasant, its NDFI exposure is entirely manageable, especially in the context of strong loss reserves (more below).

Aside from loans, Zions has a fairly large securities portfolio, though it has been gradually allowing the portfolio to roll off. As you can see, its investment securities are down about $800 million from last year. With the proceeds, Zions has been able to repay expensive wholesale borrowing and support loan growth. I expect to see some further reduction in the size of this portfolio over time. The portfolio has a 3.7-year duration and is 95% invested in U.S. government and agency debt, meaning this portfolio is not a source of credit risk.

Now, this portfolio was largely purchased when interest rates were lower, so ZION has a $1.9 billion unrealized loss on this portfolio. Its current securities yield is 2.63%. Over time, as low-yielding bonds mature and get reinvested, we should see Zion generate a higher average yield on interest-earning assets. Given the 4-year duration of the portfolio, this will be a multiyear process.

Net interest income (“NII”) grew 6% from last year to $662 million. While net interest margin (“NIM”) expanded 17 bps from last year to 3.27%, it was down 4 bps sequentially. The sequential pressure came from the fact that yields on its assets repriced more meaningfully to the Q4 rate cuts than its assets repriced. That said, over the past year it has benefited from reinvesting fixed maturities at lower rates. I expect stable to slightly better NIM from here.

Net charge-offs were just 2 bps in the quarter, and nonperforming assets are down to 48 bps of loans from 51 bps last year. During this period, it has allowed allowances to decline by 8 bps to 116 bps of loans. This still provides ~2.4x coverage of NPAs, which is well within the 200-250% coverage that I like to see. With solid reserves, ZION has the cushion to absorb unexpected underwriting hiccups, like the issue last year.

Zions maintains a strong regulatory capital position, which sits at 11.5%. As you can see below, it has steadily built capital over the past three years. This partly reflects the need to carry excess regulatory capital given the unrealized loss on its securities portfolio, which is not included in capital calculations. Adjusting for that, ZION’s capital positioning would be closer to 9.1%. Over time, I would like to see ZION run over 9.5%. Just given the rolldown in AOCI as bonds mature and pull to par, I expect adjusted CET1 to pass 9.4% by year-end. Shares offer a 2.9% dividend yield, and with its overall capital position, I view this payout as secure.

Assuming 1 Fed rate cut this year and stable credit reserves, I expect Zions to earn at least $6.30 in 2026. That leaves shares trading at about 10x earnings. As a general rule, I view 9x-12x earnings as the fair range for most regional banks, depending on their capital position, growth, and quality. ZION’s valuation has been limited by its lower adjusted CET1 ratio as well as last year’s credit loss. Now, as it retains capital and its AOCI loss slowly but steadily burns down, the capital issue disappears, which is why I expected steady multiple expansion.

Beyond this, as time goes on and Zions continues to report solid credit results, investor fear about credit losses will diminish. October will become a more distant memory, especially given the fact that ZION has manageable NDFI exposure. Over time, these two factors should enable steady multiple expansion, and I continue to view ~11x as the fair multiple. That points to shares getting past $71 over the next year. Combined with its dividend, I see shares offering a mid-teens return over the next 12 months, and I remain a "Buy" on ZION.

data.title: Ouster: Positioned For Physical AI Growth, Despite Near-Term Losses
data.description:
Ouster, Inc. (OUST) continues to grow and advance its business. The company recently completed an acquisition that can help expand its technology and also its market opportunity. The demand for automation is going to continue to grow, and Ouster has positioned itself to be a part of this growth.

The company has seen quite a bit of volatility since I reviewed the stock at the end of last year. I rated the stock a speculative buy and used that volatility to enter a position. To start the year, the stock has traded at highs around $28 a share and lows of under $17 a share. It reported strong earnings in early March, but it was not enough to lift the stock much. It has rallied as of late along with the rest of the market.

The company has shown strong revenue growth and has guided for continued revenue growth going forward. It is still operating at a large loss. This does not look like it is going to change soon. Due to the overall market strength and Ouster’s continued growth in the market, I think the stock is a speculative buy based upon the long-term trajectory of the company.

Financials

Ouster reported another strong quarter for revenue growth. The company had Q4 revenue of $62 million, an increase of 107%. This number is inflated due to a one-time royalty of $21 million. That revenue is not expected regularly. We can back that out and get revenue of $41 million, still good for growth of 36% year over year and 4% sequentially. The revenue growth was mostly driven by the industrial and robotics businesses.

The company did see a decline in the gross margin year over year; without the royalty payment, the company had a gross margin of 40%. This is down from the prior year figure of 44% and sequentially from the 42% seen in Q3. While down, the gross margin continued to be at the higher end of the range that management outlined of 35%-40% long term.

One of the big items that was a concern for me previously was related to the high operating expenses. The company has higher operating expenses than revenue; not even taking into account the gross margin, the company still would not make a profit. It will take years of revenue growth for the company to grow to a scale point sufficient to become profitable. I wanted to see the company reduce costs while still growing revenues. One of the big inputs into those costs was SBC. While this is not a cash item, it still has a cost with it. I was encouraged to see that the company reduced its costs and SBC from last quarter. The company reported operating expenses of $36.5 million in Q4 vs. $40.9 in Q3 and $38.8 in the prior year. This reduced the operating expenses to revenue from 129% in the prior year to 89% in 2025. While this number is still really high, it was a significant drop that shows the progress the company is making as it begins to scale the business. It still has a long way to go, but it is good progress. There is also a caveat in the decrease; management noted during the earnings call that they received an employment tax refund during the quarter. Some of that decline would have been due to that. Either way, the company is looking at controlling the operating expenses and working on profitability.

As noted, a big part of the expenses are related to SBC. Lowered SBC was the cause for a reduction in operating expenses for the company. SBC dropped from $11.8 million in Q3 to $7.3 million in Q4. That is a difference of $4.6 million; the operating expenses for the quarter were reduced by $4.4 million. So while the expense reductions are coming through a reduction in SBC, it still shows that the company maintained its costs during the quarter.

The company did report positive income from operations, but that was due to the one-time royalty income. If we back that out, the company had a loss from operations of $20.3 million, which is down from $24.3 million in Q3 and $25.6 million from the prior year. The company is making progress, but it still has a long way to go. At a 40% margin, the company would have to grow revenues by $50.8 million to get to a breakeven operating income at the current operating expense figure. That would be growth of 123.9%. While the company has strong revenue growth, that is still years away at the current expense rate. The company needs to continue to reduce operating expenses while maintaining revenue growth.

The company does have a strong balance sheet. The company ended the quarter with cash of $67.4 million and short-term investments of $141.2 million. Since the company does have a large SBC, the cash usage is not as high of a loss. The company reported net cash used in operating activities of $40 million for the year. That leaves the company with plenty of cash available to continue to grow the business and not worry about dilution. It also has some wiggle room if it wants to get involved in any acquisitions.

Outlook and Catalysts

Management guided for revenues of $45 million to $48 million for Q1. That would be growth of 38% to 47% from the low to high end. That would be continued strong revenue growth. Management also maintained their long-term growth estimate of 30%-50% a year going forward. That is a solid growth estimate from management.

Management also started to give more focus to becoming profitable. Controlled operating expense growth was discussed, with an estimate of a 5% to 8% increase from 2025. It is good to see management beginning to put some focus on costs and profitability. As the company begins to enter full commercialization, it needs to start running the business more efficiently. As discussed in the financial section, this likely means that the company is not going to be profitable for a few more years based upon the expense run rate. Profitability is trending in the right direction, and I do expect the cash burn and losses to continue to decrease. It is really just a matter of scaling to sufficient revenue to make up for the current expenses.

The company recently completed an acquisition of StereoLabs on February 4th. StereoLabs provides high-quality 3D vision for perception systems. StereoLabs provides AI perception software as the biggest value add to Ouster. The acquisition is combining the AI software capabilities of StereoLabs with Ouster’s digital lidar. The acquisition should help expand the market reach and addressable market for the company. The acquisition adds to the company’s abilities to advance physical AI. The company has positioned itself well for future growth within the physical AI market. I think the acquisition of StereoLabs will assist with the advancement within the industrial market. I think there is a lot of need for more automation in the industrial market, and there is strong growth potential for Ouster. StereoLabs had small revenues of $16 million in 2025, but it was EBITDA positive. So the business will be accretive right off the bat at a small scale in terms of revenues and profits.

I also think that the current estimates for the company have not baked in the acquisition of StereoLabs sufficiently yet. Analysts are expecting 2026 revenue of $218.5 million, growth of 29% from the 2025 figure. That number seems too low for me. The company just provided estimates for revenue growth of 38% on the low end and 47% on the high end. It also only includes 7 weeks of StereoLabs revenue. Starting in Q2 and forward, the financials will include a full quarter's worth of revenue from the acquisition. The company would have to see revenue slow down quite a bit in the second half of the year to not beat the estimate of 30% growth for the year. Or the company would have to miss estimates provided by management. The company has a good record of hitting revenue estimates. I think the estimate is low, and we are likely to see higher revenues for the year.

Risks

There are plenty of risks with the company. The biggest risk or concern for me is around profitability. The company continues to operate at a loss, and it does not seem that is going to change for a couple more years. While the company is making progress on their profitability, as it continues to grow, it should scale to the point where it is profitable. The concern is whether the company can maintain the growth rate needed for the extended period of time in order to become profitable. If the company is not able to execute over an extended period of time, multiple years, then profits will continue to be pushed back.

The company also operates within an increasingly competitive market. I think the company has done well to carve itself a niche by targeting a different customer base than many of the other lidar/sensor companies.

Valuation

The company continues to be a tough valuation. It is in a growing market and continues to grow revenues at a fast pace but still operates at a loss and is expected to for a while. The company is still early stage in its growth cycle, and it is valued based upon future expectations. PE or EBITDA multiples don’t have any value as the company is still not profitable.

The market cap of $1.52 billion has increased from $1.28 billion the last time I reviewed the stock. The company has also grown its revenues in that time. It now trades at a PS of 8 and a 2026 forward PS of 6.97. The multiples are very comparable to the last time I reviewed the stock. The PS ratio is not too high for a company growing revenues at over 40% a year in the latest quarter. With that kind of growth rate, it would only take a couple of years' time to see the PS multiple contract.

The concern about the valuation continues to be around profitability. The company continues to operate at a loss and will most likely for another couple of years. The long-term potential is there, but investors will have to be patient to start to see profits. The cash position is also strong, with $171 million to end the quarter. This needs to be taken into account. It also eliminates dilution risk for the most part.

Conclusion

Ouster has continued to advance and grow the business. Its technology continues to get adopted in the market. The company has also broadened its offering with the acquisition and integration of StereoLabs. I think the further expansion into the industrial market is a great move for the company that will pay future growth dividends. The further expansion into the software and becoming a more complete offering also makes the market much bigger for the company.

While the company is in a growing market and the company is seeing revenues grow, it continues to operate at a large loss. Its operating expenses continue to be nearly as high as revenue. While the company is still early in the growth stage, it is going to be multiple years before the company becomes profitable.

Overall there are concerns around profitability, but the overall trend of the company is positive. I like where the company is headed and think the future potential outweighs the risks. I continue to rate the stock a speculative buy.

data.title: Nextnav: Swings Can Be Frustrating But The FCC Is Now On Our Side
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Laughing Water Capital Q1 2026 Letter.

I have written about Nextnav (NN), our wireless spectrum / 5G terrestrial backup to GPS investment, several times in the past. This investment remains an outsized position, and it remains subject to wild swings every month around the timing of the release of each month's Federal Communications Commission (FCC) agenda. The swings can be frustrating, but in my view, the investment has been largely de-risked as the FCC submitted a draft Notice of Proposed Rule Making (NPRM) to the White House Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs (OIRA).

In other words, it seems that the FCC is now on our side.

However, the proposal still needs to pass OIRA review, which would entail checking with other agencies that could be affected by the proposal. In this case, that would primarily be the Department of Transportation (DOT) and the Department of Defense (DOD). From my perspective, it seems unlikely that FCC Chair Brendan Carr would submit a proposal to OIRA without first running it by DOT and DOD. Further, the DOT and FCC both fall under oversight from the Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee, which is chaired by Senator Ted Cruz. Cruz has been a vocal advocate for terrestrial GPS in the past, which seems to indicate that the DOT will be on board.

The DOD's spectrum usage is overseen by the National Telecommunications and Information Administration (NTIA), The NTIA is part of the Department of Commerce under Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick. Lutnick in the past has said that the government has too much spectrum, and it should be freed up for better use cases. Additionally, a backup to GPS is indeed something that the DOD wants and needs.

Lastly, recall that it was Trump who started the whole push for a backup to GPS/PNT with an executive order at the end of his first term, so it seems unlikely that an arm of the executive branch would now torpedo Nextnav's proposal.

The timing here has admittedly stretched a few months longer than I hoped for, but I think we are almost there, and I expect to see an NPRM shortly. Following an NPRM there would be a comment period that could stretch to 60 days from the time the NPRM is posted in the Federal Registrar. At this point much of what could be said has already been said during the Notice of Inquiry phase of this preceding, so I am hopeful comments will move quickly. From there I would expect a Report and Order which will allow Nextnav to use their spectrum for 5G communications and a terrestrial backup to GPS.

To be clear, we still do not know what will be in an NPRM, and it is possible that Nextnav will not get everything they have asked for, which would of course weigh on the value of the spectrum. However, the entire point of this exercise is to incentivize the mobile network operators (VZ, T, TMUS (TMUS)) or other interested parties to roll out a terrestrial backup to GPS nationwide. In order to properly motivate them, the FCC must ensure that the spectrum in question is attractively configured. If the spectrum is attractively configured, it should be quite valuable.

“How valuable” is an open question, but recent transactions suggest something north of $50 (vs. month end price of ~$15) is well within range. It is also worth noting that there is a history of spectrum pricing exceeding the top end of expectations during competitive bid processes. Recent news that Amazon (AMZN) is interested in terrestrial spectrum through Globalstar (GSAT) and that SpaceX is looking to acquire more spectrum can only be viewed as positive. Afterall, all that is needed to push value higher in an auction process of a scarce asset is two interested buyers. The arrival of two likely bidders with very deep pockets could push the price significantly higher.

data.title: MP Materials: The Market Is Missing This Rare Earth Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I approached MP Materials (MP) rather late. To be frank, I did not follow the rare earth sector at all well prior to the end of October 2025. I had underestimated how strategic a company like this could become in the American industrial landscape. The more I researched it, the clearer it became to me that there are many different aspects to this story that are greater than simply a mining story.

While I believe the market is viewing MP as far too basic, the stock has pulled back substantially from its 2025 high, and investor focus has transitioned from the geopolitical implications to near-term fundamental issues. That is exactly why I see an opportunity. The overall industrial plan has not changed, government backing remains strong and MP is already working with large industrial partners such as General Motors and Apple, alongside the Department of Defense. Given these factors combined with a nearly 40 percent decline from the highs of 2025, I find that this creates an attractive entry point for a company that is both financially de-risked and receives public support in addition to having multiple Tier-1 customers.

For me, the differentiating factor here is that MP provides both upside if prices of rare earths increase, as well as a more stable downside risk profile compared to many of its peer companies. The agreement with the Department of Defense, which includes a price floor on NdPr, has already positively impacted MP’s financial results and contributed to MP’s first positive adjusted EBITDA in some time.

I understand why the market may be skeptical regarding this company. Prior to recent developments, MP was viewed as the dominant Western-based company offering a complete solution within the sector. Today, given additional new project announcements and increasing amounts of available public money, some investors fear MP may no longer provide a distinctive contribution and therefore may lose some of its competitive advantages. For myself, I look at it in reverse. As long as there appears to be more involvement on behalf of the U.S. Government with regard to developing a domestic rare earths supply chain, then I believe the legitimacy of the entire industrial rationale becomes even stronger. Therefore, I firmly believe that the market has developed a negative view toward MP simply because MP is no longer the only entity receiving direct support. However, I would like to make clear that increased governmental support benefits all players in the sector.

Company Overview

I do not view MP Materials as a typical mining company that extracts raw materials and sells those resources to whoever offers the best price. Rather, I see it as an American industrial platform attempting to develop a complete U.S. domestic supply chain in an area where the country has been far behind for too long.

The company is currently the only fully integrated U.S. participant in the rare earths sector, having operations in all segments, including mining and processing (Mountain Pass), metallization and the production of permanent magnets. The operational center of gravity of the company is Mountain Pass, CA; additionally, MP Materials is developing the most sophisticated downstream segment located in Texas, which may have the potential to alter the financial profile of the company in the next several years.

The rest of this story is where it gets really interesting. While the marketplace has considered MP Materials as nothing more than a proxy for rare earths or merely a representation of America's need to decrease dependency on China, I believe these interpretations are now too limited. To my mind, MP Materials does not only create products, but to me it attempts to ascend the value curve.

These themes are already beginning to show themselves in the company's reported 2025 numbers. Specifically, out of total annual revenues of $224.4 million, approximately $160.4 million were derived from the Materials Segment, while $66.9 million came from the Magnetics Segment. The latter is very relevant not so much due to its present magnitude; rather, it reflects a new aspect of the story, one that has just begun (launched in mid-2025) and will ultimately be much larger should the operational ramp go as planned.

There is another thematic shift that I think needs to be completely appreciated. Previously, historically, MP was significantly more tied to China both operationally and commercially. In prior phases of this model, Shenghe represented a very significant portion of the turnover. However, in late July 2025, the company ceased product sales to China, thereby furthering the business's metamorphosis and generating a revenue base that is commensurate with an emerging American-based supply chain. Importantly, this increases the likelihood that investors who purchase shares in MP will be investing not so much as a bet on the amount of raw materials flowing into the marketplace but rather as a bet on whether or not MP can convert that raw material into strategically critical industrial outputs.

In addition to its growing use of this theme on the operational side, we are seeing evidence on the commercial side that reflects this transformation. As I said before, MP has established relationships with strategic partners like General Motors, Apple, and the Department of Defense. In short, MP is not just seeking to increase production: it is endeavoring to establish a stable presence within what has evolved into an increasingly crucial part of an industrial/political chain upon which Washington cannot allow to be determined solely by the international market.

As such, I believe MP Materials needs to be examined and evaluated in a different manner. If one views it strictly as a classic mining company, there remains ambiguity surrounding issues like cash burn rates, P/E ratios, and successful execution. On the other hand, if one views it as an industrial platform that is being developed using capital, strategic demand, and public support, then the situation changes dramatically. It does not remove uncertainty around future results, yet it helps make understanding the thesis easier. Above all else, it makes the thesis more attractive.

Earning Season

The news from the top line didn’t look great. For Q4 2025, revenues were about $52.7 million, missing the consensus estimate of about $59 million. The reason for that has been known for some time; there have been a lot of changes to the business model lately, so the way investors interpret these numbers will change as well.

The drop-off in revenue is mainly due to the strategy to get out of selling to China and lower their exposure to concentrate (a low-margin product). That’s going to cause volatility in the near term but should lead to creating a higher-margin business in the longer run.

And that’s really where the story is. Beneath the surface, in Q4 2025, MP had positive adjusted EBITDA of approximately $39 million, making it the first profitable quarter in over a year. There are several reasons for that, but the main one was the price floor mechanism related to the U.S. Department of Defense.

As you look at the EPS chart, the shift in trend is evident: After many years of sequential losses, we’re now seeing a turn toward positive territory. It may not be a smooth upward path just yet, but there is no doubt that we’ve hit an inflection point.

In addition to what’s being reported here, this also aligns with what was presented. On the “quarterly financial metrics” page, they illustrate how the increase in EBITDA came from three areas:

  • entry into magnetic products,
  • lowering unit cost,
  • price protection agreements (PPAs) contributing.

Today MP’s profit level is still influenced by policies rather than the market. In a scenario where rare earth prices continue to weaken, this policy influence is helping stabilize the company.

That said, not everything is perfect. Operating margins remain negative and highly volatile, and the chart clearly shows this. Additionally, although MP continues to generate significantly negative free cash flow during this period, those free cash flows reflect a very aggressive capital expenditures phase.

However, I don’t believe we should jump to conclusions here either; these aren’t solely operational issues, it’s capex-driven growth. MP is investing today to create capacity that, if successfully ramped up, could dramatically alter its economics over the next few years.

Ultimately (the key point), investors are still looking at dirty numbers while MP is attempting to build a set of clean numbers for the future.

Valuation

First, let us establish something simple: using the traditional multiples does not work now with MP Materials. The shares are trading at very high prices (over 200x forward P/E, and over 20x EV/Sales). However, I have stated previously that I believe those numbers are skewed because the business remains in its early stages of development. As I mentioned before, during the transitional phase of a business's development, multiples will be "high" or even "extreme".

Currently, MP Materials has a negative free cash flow and just recently became profitable again. That is why most investors will pause and conclude that the current price of the shares is too high. However, this is a static reading of a dynamic story. We should be focusing our attention on the future. In other words, MP Materials is not being priced on what it currently is today but on what it may potentially become within the next 3–5 years.

If one looks at forward estimates, the picture changes quickly:

This rapid decline in multiple values is not related to a decrease in price. Rather, the values represent increased projected earnings due to the downstream ramp, increase in volume production, and especially the growth of the magnetics segment.

Ultimately, everything relies upon the commercial model. The presentation clearly demonstrates that 2025 was a year of transition. The Company generated revenue of $224 million, while adjusted EBITDA reached positive territory at $11.4 million. Those results were largely propelled by the initiation of magnetics sales and by the contribution from the PPA.

What has yet to be incorporated into pricing expectations by the market is the next stage:

  • NdPr capacity ramp (approximately 6,000 tpa by 2026).
  • Development of the magnetics platform (Texas 10X facility).
  • Greater downstream integration.

Support provided by DoD, along with a price floor of $110/kg, provides an unusual element for a commodity story: an EBITDA floor. Therefore, this presents less downside risk than would exist for a typical producer.

My target is $100.

I simply assume that MP Materials can achieve the industrial ramp in the next 24-36 months.

I applied an implied multiple to normalized earnings (based on projections for 2027-2028), which is consistent with similar industrial companies that are growing and not pure mining companies.

As noted earlier, my valuation includes a premium for:

  • Strategic uniqueness in the U.S.
  • Government support
  • Contracts with OEMs.

I am not stating that the shares are "cheap". They are not. I do, however, feel that the market has underestimated two factors:

  • Quality of downside protection (policies + contracts)
  • Potential for margin expansion through additional downstream activities.

Conclusion

This is a complex story and one that may or may not appeal to all investors. Even though the financial picture surrounding MP continues to develop and the visibility into what is going to happen next is limited, the complexity is exactly the point.

MP’s stock price has fallen sharply from last year’s highs; yet, their overall industrial plan (and therefore government support) has been the same. Industrial agreements remain intact. And, although we are beginning to see signs of transition toward downstream in terms of the company's numbers, they are still developing.

To me, right now, the marketplace is fixated on the near term (fluctuating revenue streams and unstable margin structures) and does not adequately view the underlying structure of this story. In my opinion, MP is no longer simply a bet on rare earths. It is a bet on creating an American supply chain.

That is why I believe, at present prices, the opportunity for upside far exceeds the downside risk. That is to say, while I do not believe that the risk is low (and it certainly isn't), I believe that the potential for long-term growth, should MP execute well, is substantially underpriced.

data.title: Stride: Given Enough Time, The Problems Will Be Fixed
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Laughing Water Capital Q1 2026 Letter.

Stride (LRN) entered our portfolio as a mid-sized position. The company is the largest operator of virtual schools for grades K-12 in the U.S., serving more than 240,000 students across 30 states. Virtual schools are enjoying secular tail winds as advances in technology (Zoom, Google Meet etc.) and changes to the way online education is perceived following the Covid experience have led to what I believe is a sustainable increase in demand.

The company had been growing at mid to low teens percent for several years and traded at ~14x EBIT when they guided to 10-15% growth in enrollment for fiscal '26. However, prior to the 2025-2026 school year the company attempted to upgrade the software that governs their enrollment process as well as their learning management software. The implementation did not go smoothly, and in late October of 2025 management announced they would be cutting guidance.

In brief, the new software was disjointed to the point that parents could not successfully navigate the enrollment process, and if successful, students faced problems with logging in and finding the right classes and curriculum. This cost the company upwards of 10,000 enrollments and led to a more than 50% decline in share price, where we began to buy stock. In response to these events, management initiated a $500M buyback and moved quickly to fix the software problems.

Given that commentary from both Stride and a key competitor indicates that demand for virtual schools remains strong, this investment logically comes down to one issue; either they can fix their software, or they cannot. At present, the company is valued below 7.5x EBIT, which seems to imply either 1) that the company will not be able to fix their software or 2) that the market does not have the patience to wait for the company to fix their software.

I am of the view that given enough time, the problems will be fixed, and in fact early indications from both the company and various open market sources are that the company is well on its way down this path. As the fixes take hold, I expect shares to re-rate closer to where they traded prior to the sell off.

It is also important to note that unlike many for profit education companies that have left a sour taste in Wall Street's mouth in the past, Stride is NOT reliant on students taking on loans that they cannot afford in order to attend school. Rather, Stride enters into long term contracts with school districts who fund the students' education.

data.title: Nintendo: Mario 2, A Megahit, And Profit In J-Curve With Improvement Ahead
data.description:
Nintendo (NTDOY, NTDOF) (ISIN: JP3756600007 for TSE listing) has seen a decline in price, contrary to some of the positive headlines since our last coverage focused on the memory issue. What's new is that the Mario Galaxy Movie (Mario 2) is looking like it will pass $1 billion in gross, which means it is close to matching the contribution of the first movie, which we believe means it will contribute a ~12% profit growth vector in next year's results. This continues to bode well for the Zelda movie, coming next year, which we think can contribute similarly as it's a similarly important Nintendo franchise. On the other hand, since our last coverage, there was also the negative news that the Switch 2 production forecasts had been cut due to somewhat underwhelming holiday sales, but we highlight that having already outsold the Switch 1 in a similar timeframe, it's not a disappointment to us also, as hardware has poorer unit economics in this latest console compared to the previous one, particularly with the lower-priced region-locked region driving sales. But on software, Pokopia was a stealth hit despite not being a mainline Pokémon game, meaning more high-margin software contribution is incoming in results.

Our thesis continues to be that future software sales will drive the mix and margin and that this counteracts the fact that region-locked Japanese models need several per-head software sales to break even. Pokopia will help bridge the gap (maybe a new Fire Emblem game too), so we could even see some unexpected short-term margin support as we wait for when more major titles start to contribute to revenue, like Pokémon Winds and Waves, expected in 2027. But we do want to mention that price pressure for Nintendo could last until 2026 even with a strong movie release, absent any major title releases, which may start in earnest in 2027 as the major catalyst. But we'd rather entrench our position now than later.

Puts and Takes for Upcoming Earnings and Beyond

Nintendo's last release was for the 9m, with the fiscal year ending at the end of March. The major unexpected thing that will be contributing within the fiscal year's close is Pokopia. It sold 2.2 million units in 4 days from its release, which is phenomenal for a non-mainline Pokémon title with a Minecraft gimmick. Usually, the first month of sales of a title is about half of what a title will sell in its first year. We have that JP sales of Pokopia are a little over 800k in the first month. Presuming the ratio of Nintendo's overall sales of 1:3 in JP versus the rest of the world applies to Pokopia, we estimate 3.3 million sales in one month, which will contribute to the coming results, and approximately another 3.3 million after that, contributing to the next fiscal year. Pokopia sells at about 8.5k JPY at Yodobashi, so crudely maybe around $40 per unit in wholesale through retail channels assuming retail markups - though Nintendo sells plenty direct through the Pokémon centre and with digital sales - so 198 million JPY in software sales just from Pokopia. Those units alone add 10% to the 9m Switch 2 software unit sales and a little under 3% to the overall unit sales, including on Switch 1.

We expect operating profit to accelerate on Pokopia's software contribution. This is because a lot of the growth in the Switch 2 console sales is happening at not great margins, so any high-margin unit contribution will be noticed in the results. Part of the reason for the exceptional opening of the Switch 2, beating out the Switch 1 in sales milestones, is because of the Japanese region-locked model. Japan was the relatively strong negative geographic mix component in the last results as well, and the reason is that the region-locked model is a lot cheaper and meant just for the Japanese market at an affordable price; therefore, it is in high demand. The region-locked model also contributes negatively to the mix because of the weaker JPY, with parts sourced from abroad. They actually make a loss per region-locked unit, which is not Nintendo's traditional model where they usually sell consoles at a slight profit - we think the international Switch 2 sales are at a profit. On average, they should still be making a profit on the Switch 2 for now, before taking into account possible memory price increases once supply agreements need to be rolled over. But for the region-locked model, they need to be able to sell about 3 software titles before they are making a profit on the person who bought that console. It's very doable, given that median game ownership is around 35 according to some surveys.

In terms of the Switch 2 console sales, in our last coverage it was around 17 million cumulatively; based on the latest Japan data, we estimate around 20 million cumulatively, so about 20% sequential growth from the 9m cumulative to the 12-month figure. Since the console has been out for 9 months as of the end of March, there is a sequential deceleration showing, which should shift the margin profile upwards in the FY results.

For beyond the end of the fiscal year results, the next quarterly result will include the Mario 2 movie, which, at a predicted terminal $1 billion at the box office and therefore around $750 million in profit, we expect around 60 million in ordinary profit contribution (50% financed by partners) and therefore around a 50% profit boost to Q1 (call it around ~12% for the next FY by annualising), compared to the average quarterly ordinary profit based on the latest 9-month results. It's a significant boost, in addition to the trickle of Pokopia sales after its first month's sales surge, which will contribute to the FY ending in March earnings release in May. We also expect in 2026 a Fire Emblem game, where the last game sold 1.6 million in its first quarter and the previous game 2.29 million. It won't be as big as Pokopia, but it will have a higher margin than the console sales and shield results until calendar 2027, when new mainline Pokémon games will be released that are Switch 2 exclusives that should drive both new platform adoption more internationally, therefore a positive mix effect on hardware, which is more profitable in international geographies, and also in terms of overall positive mix effects from software versus sales.

Bottom Line

The FY should see some margin lift from Pokopia software sales, but we expect Japan to remain strong in the mix for hardware. In the next fiscal year, we expect margins to lift further from the latter half of the Pokopia sales curve, the megahit Mario 2 movie, maybe a Fire Emblem game, and also perhaps less Switch 2 unit sales than expected (therefore less hardware contribution overall due to frontloading), though possibly with a more international positive mix effect on hardware with the JP hardware sales now frontloaded. Maybe the next Pokémon mainline games will release within March-end 2027, in which case a strong software and margin lift can be expected, as well as upgrades by Switch 1 players to the latest console, eager to keep up with the Pokémon juggernaut franchise - if not in the fiscal year after. A new mainline game (based on Scarlet and Violet sales) should exceed both the Legends: ZA game released this fiscal year (over 12 million units as of the 9m) as well as the first half of Pokopia's sales curve (estimated 3.3 million units). We do believe that Nintendo profits are in a J-curve, and as software picks up in the mix and international Switch 2 sales grow in the mix too, with the Zelda movie coming in the calendar 2027 as well, operating profit growth should be notable to catalyse the stock price, which languishes for now. With a trailing PE of 25x, close to the lower trailing PE levels of the last 5 years, despite considerable software and IP profit driving ahead of it with Mario 2 already banked and Zelda next year, we think Nintendo is a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
